request.question: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?

title: Catfish Creek (Iowa)
Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River)
Rainwater Creek
Passage Creek
North Fork Flathead River
Black Creek (Florida)
Smith Creek (Virginia)
Shenandoah River
Los Baños Creek
Yager Creek
sentences: Catfish Creek is a 21.2 mi tributary of the upper Mississippi River in northeastern Iowa.
It rises southwest of Dubuque, just southeast of Peosta.
The Middle Fork rises west of Dubuque and flows under and then parallel to U.S. Route 20; the former Illinois Central Railroad, now the Chicago Central and Pacific Railroad has its mainline in it.
The remaining forks of the creek are all south of U.S. Route 52.
Swiss Valley Park, maintained by Dubuque County, is along the main fork of the creek, which is a designated trout stream through the park.
In recent years, people have been trying to stop the erosion in this relatively flat way out of the Gorge of the Upper Mississippi.
The North Fork of the creek has been proposed for a trail/park.
Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.
It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.
Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.
Rainwater Creek is a 3.9 mi tributary stream of Lake Fork Creek.
It flows into Lake Fork Creek downstream from the Lake Fork Reservoir Dam approximately four miles west of Quitman in western Wood County, Texas.
Its primary source is a spring, augmented by stormwater runoff.
It is named for the family of William Walton Rainwater, who was among the earliest permanent settlers of the area.
Passage Creek is a 38.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in Fort Valley, Virginia.

For most of its length it flows through a rural valley between the two spine-like ridges of Massanutten Mountain, then exits the valley by cutting a narrow gorge through the northeast end of the mountain.
The North Fork Flathead River (Ktunaxa: "kqaskanmituk" ) is a 153 mi river flowing through British Columbia, Canada, south into the U.S. state of Montana.
It is one of the three primary forks of the Flathead River, the main inflow of Flathead Lake and a tributary of the Columbia River via the Clark Fork River and the Pend Oreille River.
The river is sometimes considered the upper headwaters of the Flathead River, although the North Fork is its official name in the U.S.
Other naming conventions for the river include Flathead River - North Fork, North Fork of Flathead River, and North Fork of the Flathead River.
Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.
It is located in Clay County, Florida.
Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.
North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.
North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.
South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.
The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.
Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.
Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.
Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.
The Shenandoah River is a tributary of the Potomac River, 55.6 mi long with two forks approximately 100 mi long each, in the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.

The principal tributary of the Potomac, the river and its tributaries drain the central and lower Shenandoah Valley and the Page Valley in the Appalachians on the west side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, in northwestern Virginia and the Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia.
Los Baños Creek or Los Banos Creek, originally El Arroyo de los Baños, is a tributary stream of the San Joaquin River.
Its source drains the slopes of the Diablo Range within the Central Valley of California, United States.
Los Baños Creek has its source at the confluence of North Fork Los Banos Creek and South Fork Los Banos Creek.
It flows northeast to the west edge of Los Banos and then north to Mud Slough 2.5 miles (4 km) upstream from its confluence with the San Joaquin River.
Yager Creek is a tributary stream of the Van Duzen River on the north coast of California in Humboldt County, California.
It has its source at the confluence of North Fork Yager Creek and Middle Fork Yager Creek.
Its mouth is at the confluence with the Van Duzen River just below the town of Carlotta.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?
hop1_docs: Smith Creek (Virginia) | Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.  Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.  Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.

Hayfork Creek | Hayfork Creek is a tributary of the South Fork Trinity River in Northern California in the United States.  At over 50 mi long, it is the river's longest tributary and is one of the southernmost streams in the Klamath Basin.  It winds through a generally steep and narrow course north then west through the forested Klamath Mountains, but also passes through the Hayfork and Hyampom Valleys, which comprise the primary agricultural regions of Trinity County.

North Fork Embarras River | The North Fork Embarras River is a 64.0 mi stream which originates in Edgar County, Illinois, and flows through Clark, Crawford and Jasper counties.  Its confluence with the Embarras River is located near the town of Ste. Marie in Jasper County.  It is a major tributary to the Embarras River.  The North Fork's main tributary is Panther Creek, located in Jasper County.

Black Creek (Florida) | Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.  It is located in Clay County, Florida.  Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.  North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.  North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.  South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.  The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.

North Fork Smith River (California) | The North Fork Smith River is 28 mi tributary of the Smith River that begins in the U.S. state of Oregon and ends in the U.S. state of California.  Arising near Chetco Peak in the Klamath Mountains, it flows generally south to meet the Middle Fork Smith River at Gasquet, California.  The combined streams form the Smith River.

Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River) | Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.  It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.  Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.

South Fork Crooked River | The South Fork Crooked River is a tributary, 76 mi long, of the Crooked River in the U.S. state of Oregon.  Starting southeast of Hampton in Deschutes County, the South Fork flows east from near Ram Lake (dry).  It passes under U.S. Route 20 at Hampton, then continues east and north for about 6 mi before entering Crook County.  Shortly thereafter, east of Hampton Buttes, it receives Buck Creek from the right and then passes through Logan Reservoir.  Flowing north, it receives Sand Hollow Creek from the left, then Twelvemile Creek from the right before crossing under Oregon Route 380 (Southeast Paulina Highway).  Just north of the highway, the river joins Beaver Creek to form the Crooked River 125 mi from its confluence with the Deschutes River.
response.summary_1: Evidence: "Smith Creek ... is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River" and is located "in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia." Bridge entity: the North Fork of the Shenandoah River (i.e., the Shenandoah River). Missing fact / next-hop direction: retrieve the Shenandoah River (or "North Fork Shenandoah River") article to find which U.S. state(s) the Shenandoah River "passes through" or "flows through" (search for phrases like "flows through" or "states" on the Shenandoah River page).
hop2_docs: Lynch River | The Lynch River is a 9.8 mi tributary of the North Fork Rivanna River in the U.S. state of Virginia.  It is part of the James River watershed.  It rises at the unincorporated community of Mission Home near the boundary of Shenandoah National Park and flows southeast past Shady Grove and Nortonsville to join the Roach River, forming the North Fork of the Rivanna at their confluence.  The Lynch River flows through Greene and Albemarle counties, crossing their boundary several times.

Shenandoah Creek | Shenandoah Creek is a tributary of Mahanoy Creek in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, in the United States.  It is at least 5 mi long and flows through Shenandoah, West Mahanoy Township, Butler Township, and Girardville.

Little River (North River) | Two streams named Little River flow to the North River, a tributary of the South Fork Shenandoah River in the U.S. state of Virginia.  Both rivers flow within the George Washington National Forest.

Thornton River | The Thornton River is a 27.9 mi river in the U.S. state of Virginia.  It rises at Thornton Gap in Shenandoah National Park and flows east through Rappahannock County, running parallel to U.S. Route 211 until it reaches the town of Sperryville.  Continuing east into Culpeper County, the Thornton River joins the Hazel River, a tributary of the Rappahannock River, and thus part of the Chesapeake Bay watershed.

German River | The German River is the principal tributary of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River, flowing for 14.7 mi in the U.S. state of Virginia.  The river originates in northern Rockingham County, just east of the West Virginia border, in the George Washington National Forest, near the crest of Shenandoah Mountain in the Allegheny Mountains.

Rapidan River | The Rapidan River, flowing 88 mi through north-central Virginia in the United States, is the largest tributary of the Rappahannock River.  The two rivers converge just west of the city of Fredericksburg.  The Rapidan River begins west of Doubletop Mountain in Shenandoah National Park where the Mill Prong meets the Laurel Prong at Rapidan Camp, approximately 3 mi south of Big Meadows.

Dry River (Virginia) | The Dry River is a 22.6 mi tributary of the North River in the U.S. state of Virginia.  It flows entirely within Rockingham County, rising within the George Washington National Forest east of the West Virginia border and flowing south to the North River at Bridgewater.  Via the North River, it is part of the Shenandoah River system, flowing to the Potomac River.
response.summary_2: Smith Creek is a 35.5‑mi tributary of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia. The supplied passages discuss Virginia-area tributaries but do not state which U.S. state(s) the Shenandoah River passes through.
